content: The 11:00 PM lull has hit, that soft bruise of an hour where the dinner crowd has cleared out and the serious drinkers haven't yet reached their desperation. I pull my hands from the keys, letting the last chord—a bruised B-flat major with a sharp eleventh—hang in the humid air of Sheba’s. It’s a stubborn sound, refusing to dissipate, much like the smell of injera and expensive bourbon that clings
